FT_IC1.evidence: Data from U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, data release 5.0, 21 data collection sites across the United States, children enrolled ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis of ABCD participants followed annually for 4 years using linear mixed models and cross-lagged panel models.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatrics Open Science, Volume 2, Issue 1, 2026. No indication of duplicate or preprint status.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social media use (items 4+5+6: texting + social networking sites + video chat) is a qualifying phone/SM-specific variable with its own beta coefficients throughout Table 2 (e.g., social media use x time for inattention: β [SE] = 0.03 [0.01], P<.001) and cumulative effect β=0.15 predicting ADHD inattention symptoms (individual-trait outcome). SM is IV; inattention/ADHD symptoms are individual-trait DV.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple standardized β coefficients with SEs and p-values reported throughout Table 2 and Figure 3 (cross-lagged panel models); cross-lagged β=0.03, p=.004 for SM→inattention.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with quantitative results for the SM↔ADHD symptom relationship reported in main text and supplemental tables.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media use is the IV (predictor) in the main longitudinal analyses predicting ADHD/inattention symptoms (DV). EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis; that condition is not met here.
notes: Social media composite = texting + social networking sites + video chat (items 4+5+6 of Youth Screen Time Survey). Video games and TV/videos analyzed separately and do not carry IC2 independently, but the SM composite alone meets IC2. Cross-lagged panel models additionally confirm SM as IV predicting inattention.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study Release 5.1, 11,867 children recruited across 21 sites in the US, baseline ages 9-10 (Y0) and two-year follow-up (Y2; ages 11-12).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal neuroimaging analysis using resting-state and MID task fMRI from ABCD Study to predict addictive screen use outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal pre-proof accepted March 11, 2026 in Biological Psychiatry: Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging. No indication this is a duplicate of an already-included peer-reviewed paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: addictive social media use (SMAQ-based questionnaire administered at Y2) and addictive phone use are analyzed separately with their own β coefficients (e.g., Figure 1: β_M1=-.030, N.S. for addictive SM use; β_M1=-.039, p=.035 for addictive phone use). These are qualifying SM/phone-specific variables (addictive SM use scale; addictive phone use scale). The other side of the relationship is neural (cortical connectomic maturation AFC score and NAc activation) which qualifies as individual-trait (neural).

FT_IC3.evidence: Standardized β coefficients with 95% CIs and permutation-based p-values reported throughout (e.g., max-ASU: β_M1=-.056, CI=[-.083,-.027], p<.001; addictive videogaming: β_M1=-.060, CI=[-.087,-.031], p<.001; NAc→max-ASU: β_M2=-.023, CI=[-.045,-.000], p=.029).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential regression and mediation analyses with numeric quantitative results reported throughout for SM/phone use outcomes.

FT_EC7_reason: All three EC7 conditions hold: (a) longitudinal design (Y0 neural measures predict Y2 ASU outcomes); (b) SM/phone use (addictive social media use, addictive phone use) is the DV in every analysis involving these variables — neural variables are always IV and ASU measures are always DV; (c) there is no analysis anywhere in the paper with SM/phone as IV. There is also no cross-sectional analysis with SM as DV because ASU questionnaires were not administered at Y0 baseline in the ABCD Study — only longitudinal Y0→Y2 analyses are present. Cross-sectional carve-out does not apply.
notes: Addictive videogaming (VGAQ-based) is the primary qualifying variable showing significant results; addictive social media use and addictive phone use are also separately analyzed with their own coefficients. All ASU measures are exclusively DV throughout. Paper explicitly notes ASU questionnaires were first available at Y2, confirming no cross-sectional ASU analyses are possible.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from ABCD Study Version 5.1 (Garavan et al., 2018), population-based cohort of 11,868 children aged 9-10 at baseline, recruited from 22 research sites across the United States.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using ABCD Study follow-up data at years 2 and 3 (ages 12-13) using linear mixed models with random intercepts.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 15 (2026) 1, 320-331. No indication of duplicate status.

FT_IC2.evidence: The primary exposure is problematic gaming measured with the Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale). Per binding clarification 2, gaming-only scales (VGAQ, problematic gaming without explicit mobile/phone modality) fail IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable with its own coefficient. Problematic social media use (6-item SMAQ from Bagot et al., 2022) appears in the paper but only as a covariate/adjustment variable in sensitivity analyses testing robustness of the problematic gaming→PLEs association — it is added to the model as a covariate to control for overlap with gaming, not analyzed as IV or DV with its own primary coefficient table. The discussion notes problematic SMU was 'associated with higher PLEs at year 3' but this is from a sensitivity model where SMAQ adjusts the gaming effect, not a model where SMAQ is the focal IV with a separately reported coefficient meeting Step 1. No separate analysis reports a numeric estimate where SMAQ is the focal IV paired with an individual-trait outcome. Gaming-only exposure without a separately estimable phone/SM variable → NOT_MET.

notes: Core focus is problematic gaming (VGAQ) → psychotic-like experiences (PLEs). Gaming alone fails IC2. Problematic SMU appears in the paper but as a covariate in sensitivity analyses testing whether gaming→PLEs persists after adjusting for SMAQ, and as an additional variable mentioned in discussion. The paper states analyses were conducted in 'the subsample of 3,129 participants who reported using social media' adjusting for SMAQ, but this is an adjustment covariate model, not a primary or supplementary model estimating SMAQ→PLEs as the focal relationship. No separate table or figure reports SMAQ as focal IV with its own numeric estimate.
